reasoning:
The threshold to exceed is tightly anchored to ~59.3 fatalities per 30-day period. This historically high average is heavily inflated by the massive post-election protests from late 2024 to early 2025, which accounted for approximately 300 extra fatalities. Removing this one-off political spike brings the baseline fatality rate of the long-running Islamist insurgency in Cabo Delgado closer to 30-40 fatalities per month. 

For the nearest resolution date (2025-09-07), the 30-day window covers August 8 to September 7. ACLED reports and other monitoring sources confirm that August 2025 has been quite calm, with known fatalities likely tracking well under 20 for the August 8-31 period. Ergo, it would mathematically require an immediate, massive casualty event (~40+ fatalities) in the first 7 days of September to surpass the threshold, which is highly improbable (P = 0.06).

For the remaining resolution dates (Sep 30, Nov 29, Feb 2026), each corresponds to a completely unobserved 30-day window. Given the underlying right-skewed distribution of conflict fatalities, routine insurgency violence will likely fall short of the 59.3 mark (most months will be in the 20-40 range). However, Islamic State Mozambique (ISM) remains active, and sudden major clashes (such as the Macomia assault in May 2024) can independently pull a month over 60. 

As the forecast horizon naturally extends, I apply a progressively increasing uncertainty premium. Over longer horizons, new unpredictable variables (e.g., renewed political protests, unexpected offensives by the reinforced 5,000-strong Rwandan force, or seasonal weather impacts) widen the distribution of specific monthly fatality outcomes. Therefore, probability scales up from 0.16 to 0.32.
